title: Atmospheric and meteorological responses during the April 8, 2024, total solar eclipse: advancing workforce development through experiential learning
institutions: Cooperative Institute for Mesoscale Meteorological Studies; University of Maryland, Baltimore County
topics: Atmospheric Ozone and Climate; Atmospheric aerosols and clouds; Climate variability and models
abstract: On 8 April 2024, a total solar eclipse affected the eastern United States, creating natural experiment to examine short-term atmospheric responses an abrupt reduction in radiation. We collected observations at Howard University Beltsville Research Campus (HUBC; 39.05° N, 76.88° W), where ground-based and upper-air measurements captured pre-, during-, post-eclipse conditions under mostly clear skies. At HUBC, peak obscuration reached ∼89% 15:20 EDT. Downwelling shortwave irradiance decreased from clear-sky values &gt;900 W m -2 &lt;20 (≈98% reduction). Near-surface air temperature declined 16.3 °C 13.5 (−2.8 °C), lagging maximum by ∼10 min, while relative humidity increased ∼28% ∼34% (a ∼22% increase), consistent with cooling. Surface wind speeds weakened ∼1 s -1 . HALO Doppler lidar indicated suppressed vertical velocity variance (SD (w) ∼0.5–0.8 peak), reduced turbulent mixing transient stabilization of convective boundary layer. Microwave radiometer profiles showed cooling strongest near surface diminished lapse rates aloft. These findings are eclipse-induced lower atmosphere disruption normal diurnal cycle. Representing detailed case study Mid-Atlantic, our results contribute growing body meteorology literature. This effort also served as powerful STEM education opportunity, engaging students hands-on fieldwork data collection, thereby highlighting value events laboratories for both scientific investigation public engagement.

title: Atmospheric and meteorological responses during the April 8, 2024, total solar eclipse: advancing workforce development through experiential learning

abstract: On 8 April 2024, a total solar eclipse affected the eastern United States, creating natural experiment to examine short-term atmospheric responses an abrupt reduction in radiation. We collected observations at Howard University Beltsville Research Campus (HUBC; 39.05° N, 76.88° W), where ground-based and upper-air measurements captured pre-, during-, post-eclipse conditions under mostly clear skies. At HUBC, peak obscuration reached ∼89% 15:20 EDT. Downwelling shortwave irradiance decreased from clear-sky values >900 W m⁻² <20 (≈98% reduction). Near-surface air temperature declined 16.3 °C 13.5 (−2.8 °C), lagging maximum by ∼10 min, while relative humidity increased ∼28% ∼34% (a ∼22% increase), consistent with cooling. Surface wind speeds weakened ∼1 m s⁻¹. HALO Doppler lidar indicated suppressed vertical velocity variance (SD (w) ∼0.5–0.8 s⁻¹ peak), reduced turbulent mixing transient stabilization of convective boundary layer. Microwave radiometer profiles showed cooling strongest near surface diminished lapse rates aloft. These findings are eclipse-induced lower atmosphere disruption normal diurnal cycle. Representing detailed case study Mid-Atlantic, our results contribute growing body meteorology literature. By utilizing robust libraries pandas, matplotlib, seaborn, the effectively mitigates inconsistencies promotes reproducibility. The findings highlight significant improvements quality, allowing more accurate identification of trends across troposphere stratosphere. abstract: Developing business-logic-rich microservices requires navigating complex trade-offs between data consistency and distributed coordination. Current architectural simulators prioritize network metrics over domain semantics, whereas industry frameworks demand full-scale infrastructure deployments, preventing early experimentation. To bridge this gap, we introduce a \textit{Domain-Driven Design} (DDD) microservice simulator that isolates core business logic from communication transactional infrastructure. By modeling systems around aggregates, the allows developers evaluate identical application code under varying guarantees constraints. It features support for multiple models (Sagas, TCC) seamless transitions across diverse deployment topologies, ranging centralized execution fully environments. title: Analysis, design and implementation of a residential inductive contactless energy transfer system with multiple mobile clamps
institutions: Momeneh, Arash; Castilla Fernández, Miguel; Moradi Ghahderijani, Mohammad; Miret Tomàs, Jaume; García de Vicuña Muñoz de la Nava, José Luis; Universitat Politècnica de Catalunya
abstract: This study presents the analysis, design and implementation of a simple and cost-effective residential inductive contactless energy transfer system with multiple mobile clamps. The topology is based on the cascaded connection of a buck converter and a high-frequency resonant inverter loaded by several output passive rectifiers. The proposed system includes a sliding transformer to supply the mobile loads, leading to a safe and flexible location of loads. The theoretical analysis and design of the proposed system is based on a mathematical model derived using the first harmonic approximation. Selected experimental results are included to verify the system features. In comparison with conventional topology, the proposed system significantly improves efficiency, complexity and cost.
